Subject: PAE
Familiarity with V, CV, VC: I can recognize them without a chart. I can also find them with a chart.
Familiarity with logograms: I know a few by memory.
H Easiest aspects: Mastering the basic strokes (h v, d, etc.)
H Worst aspects: Paying attention to the wrong details, e.g., if one stroke was shorter than the other, and trying to code it even if it wasn't necessary for decipherment.
H Suggested improvements: A longer training session with basic signs first, then getting more difficult, perhaps production-focused: "Make this sign", to get to know the mechanics of Hantatallas better.
H Other information: Some of the aesthetic options may not be used (or necessary) for the experiment. Should they be included in the experiment, even though they are great for more experienced users?
Z Easiest aspects: Working with characters that were damaged on the right side
Z Worst aspects: Working with characters that were damaged on the left side, differentiating different signs that started with two horizontal strokes.
Z Suggested improvements: A table organized by right side of character

Subject: PBE
Familiarity with logograms: Very little, except for a handful of common ones.
H Worst aspects: Figuring out which parts to focus on
Z Easiest aspects: Recognizing the signs in the index once I'd found them
Z Worst aspects: Searching through the index when I couldn't figure out what pattern of leftmost strokes it wanted (or when I had no clear leftmost strokes)
Z Suggested improvements: An index at the beginning showing stroke patterns and which page they begin on

Subject: PA1
Familiarity with V, CV, VC: I could about a year ago when I took the class and have not tried since. If I have a chart, I think I will be able to find them quickly.
Familiarity with CVC: Again, haven't tried in a while, but can find them using a chart.
Familiarity with logograms: I vaguely remember the concept of logograms, but am not experienced with recognizing them.
Familiarity with the Zeichenlexikon: That name sounds familiar, but I believe that I never used the resource.
Familiarity with Hantatallas: I used that a few times during my time in LING199.
Other information: I was in LING199 (Hittite Language and Culture) with Prof. Shosted in Spring 2021.
H Easiest aspects: This system is very easy to use when there are only a few elements in the sign.
H Worst aspects: Positioning elements in the right places. I am sometimes unsure how to produce the positioning I want using the syntax available.
H Suggested improvements: I think a drag and drop feature for placing sign elements on a canvas could be useful for signs that have complex positioning.
H Other information: I think it is a great system. Potentially adding real pictures to each entry could make the user more certain of their identification.
Z Easiest aspects: When the signs started with a rarer beginning like a winklehacken this was an easy system to use.
Z Worst aspects: When a sign was similar to the one being identified, other similar signs weren't necessarily right next to it. In addition, it was sometimes hard when the beginning of the sign was unclear or damaged.
Z Suggested improvements: More organization schemes where signs could be listed multiple times. For example, it could list signs by the left-most stoke and then in another section sort by right-most stroke to help identify damaged/unclear signs.
Z Other information: This system is much more frustrating because it involves scrolling through and looking at many signs that aren't even close to the one you are looking for.

Subject: PB1
Familiarity with V, CV, VC: I can find them on a chart, but I can only recognize a few V signs without one.
Familiarity with CVC: I do not remember much at all.
Familiarity with the Zeichenlexikon: I remember talking about them in class and how to generally use them.
Familiarity with Hantatallas: It would come back to me fairly quickly once I use it again.
H Easiest aspects: Formatting the strokes was pretty straightforward.
H Worst aspects: Trying different combinations of signs and figuring out differences between strokes.
H Suggested improvements: A sketchpad that converts your drawing to possible signs would be pretty cool.
Z Worst aspects: Figuring out the organizational patterns and not being able to only search for parts of signs.
Z Suggested improvements: More clear ways to order the signs or smaller categories.

Subject: PA2
Familiarity with V, CV, VC: I can recognize them with a chart with a general refresher
Familiarity with CVC: I can recognize them with a chart with a general refresher
Familiarity with logograms: I can recognize them with a chart with a general refresher
Familiarity with the Zeichenlexikon: I have heard of it and used it (or a similar tool) briefly
H Easiest aspects: Strokes stacked or next to each other were easy to find.  I also liked how you could sort the signs that matched by complexity or frequency.
H Worst aspects: Trying to remember the coding language was a bit difficult/draining, but gets easier as you go on.  Also, it was difficult when no signs came up, even though the rendering looked like a sign.
H Suggested improvements: This system could have a more approachable/welcoming visual interface, which would make it look less technical and less intimidating. Also, the search results could be adjusted a bit to include "could be" signs when no results match the specific code sequence (for example, signs with all but 1 of the code as the same).
H Other information: I used this regularly when I took LING 199, so I was more familiar with it than noted in the pre survey - I had forgotten what it was called.
Z Easiest aspects: It was helpful to see so many images, and to see similar images to the sign I was looking for and compare them
Z Worst aspects: There were so many signs to go through, and such slight differences, that it was exhausting and took forever to find the correct sign.  Also, it didn't work when the left side was incomplete or missing.
Z Suggested improvements: Being able to click a link to the number from the sign would have made this way easier than scrolling through
Z Other information: I really like how many images there are, and feel the "realistic" look of the images helped me be a bit more accurate and confident in my answers

Subject: PB2
Outside experience: No, just at the University of Illinois.
Familiarity with V, CV, VC: Probably ok with a chart, too rusty to recognize without a chart.
Familiarity with the Zeichenlexikon: Assuming the searchable version, fairly famliar.
H Easiest aspects: Identifying the parts I was confident in in terms of stroke type, matching the signs/strokes to the search results.
H Worst aspects: Had a pretty big learning curve getting comfortable with the formulae, and I really wouldn't have survived without the real-time graph updates and the debugging tips. Another thing that also affected my overall confidence in identifications was the possibility of different code combinations that looked similar having different search results.
H Suggested improvements:
1. Perhaps having a filter of left/right side would help for some larger signs? For example, sometimes I could see something like a 'pa' on the right half of the sign, but I'd have to scroll until I saw results that had it there.

2. Not sure if this is statistically possible, but maybe having suggestions of other code combinations that kind of look like what I put in would be helpful too.
Z Easiest aspects: The Winkelhaken section of the table in particular worked great. The handwritten style also made some signs easy to spot.
Z Worst aspects: It was a lot of scrolling and loading, and the indexing sequence in the table took some getting used to (I had to remember that the signs weren't organized in terms of complexity).
Z Suggested improvements: Definitely a key/table of contents that shows how the Table is organized (e.g., single horizontal >> double horizontal but top short >> etc,), and it would be great if there was a way to skip to sign numbers just by clicking it.
Z Other information: I actually found some signs by trying to find combinations on the right side, or the end of the sign. The indexing in the table became less effective when I did that, but it could still narrow things down a little most of the time.

Subject: AX1
Familiarity with V, CV, VC: I can generally recognise them without much trouble without the help of a chart, though sometimes I make mistakes
Familiarity with CVC: I'm less familiar with CVC signs, and can recognise about half of them without a chart.
Familiarity with logograms: I am familiar with only a handful of logograms.
Familiarity with the Zeichenlexikon: I have not used sign lists before, except for a couple of times in the Introduction to Hittite sign list while doing assignments.
Familiarity with Hantatallas: I have used the Hantatallas system a few times while doing transliteration experiments
H Easiest aspects: I liked that there was a recommendation system, where a lot of signs were listed out
H Worst aspects: Distinguishing between c and d/h signs was sometimes difficult, and sometimes the signs weren't easy to map out with the provided language - though partial matching was a godsend
Z Easiest aspects: Once the sign was found, it was easy to jump to the entry
Z Worst aspects: Identifying the sign itself, especially when it was damaged or complex, or otherwise hard to identify the first stroke(s)
Z Suggested improvements: If the signs were ordered within the categorisations in a finer way

Subject: BY1

Familiarity with CVC: decently familiar. generally recognizable without a chart, and can be found quickly with a chart
Familiarity with logograms: not very familiar, can only recognize a few, but given a chart can recognize some

Subject: AY1
Familiarity with V, CV, VC: Can find quickly with a chart. Used to be able to recognize the most used signs
H Easiest aspects: using the syntax to build the signs
H Worst aspects: finding partial signs system seems to struggle with those a bit
H Other information: doesn't always do well with only parts of a sign entered
Z Easiest aspects: finding the sign number page after locating the sign

Subject: CY1
Familiarity with V, CV, VC: I am very familiar with V, CV, and VC signs and can accurately recognize them without a chart. I am also familiar with the locations of these signs and how to find them on a chart.
Familiarity with CVC: I am familiar with all of the CVC signs, but I don't have the same level of confidence with memorization, although I do have a fair amount of them memorized.
Familiarity with logograms: I am fairly familiar with logograms, but as with the CVC a chart is fairly helpful and I know how to find them quickly given a chart.
Familiarity with the Zeichenlexikon: I am familiar with the sign list in the back of our textbook from this semester's class which I honestly prefer using rather than the chart handouts. I find them relatively easy to navigate in general.
H Easiest aspects: It was helpful to be able to enter the parts of the signs that could be identified (especially when there was damage in the autograph). It narrowed the search down enough (~10-40 signs) so that the options could be scrolled through quickly.
H Worst aspects: Sometimes there were a lot of given options when only part of the sign was entered, especially if it was a relatively simple sign or not many strokes could be identified.
H Suggested improvements: I would have liked images in the system to be actual autographs rather than computer generated. I get for identification purposes, computer generated results are needed but if the images from the textbook could be alongside the options, that would be very helpful.
H Other information: In general this system was much easier to use than the textbook, and I like how you don't have to code the exact sign to yield possible results, which helps with signs that are not completely identifiable due to damage.
Z Easiest aspects: Similar signs were located near each other, so if certain strokes were hard to identify in the image, I could compare possibilities from the textbook to find the most accurate. This was especially helpful for the autographs that had damage or were missing part of the sign.
Z Worst aspects: There were so many signs in the list it was sometimes difficult to locate the sign quickly, even when I knew almost exactly what I was looking for.
Z Suggested improvements: I liked how the book was organized by the leftmost stroke(s) but I wish it were more clear how the signs were organized after that. I caught on to some of the patterns, but it did take a while.
Z Other information: This system definitely works, but I think the main issue is that it is so time consuming. To identify signs by looking at a textbook, I think this is about as efficient as we can hope for.

Subject: BX1
H Easiest aspects: Formatting the elements and manipulating how they had to be placed
H Worst aspects: Finding the figure from the search results when only part of the word was looked up. Some search results probably could have been filtered out.
H Suggested improvements: Sometimes, a similar figure showed up in the search results that would have taken me a while to format. Being able to get the sign from any of the words in the search results would allow me to modify that to fit the sign I was searching for.
Z Easiest aspects: The only somewhat easy part was figuring out the location of the starting stroke in the table. Even so, it was unclear how many words started with the same starting stroke. For example, for the two horizontal strokes and the two horizontal strokes that were staggered--it seemed like the actual words in the table were unclear about the placement.
Z Worst aspects: There was simply no order to how the symbols were placed. Even the major categories of starting symbols were haphazard, which made it very difficult to use. Looking up actual symbols was very tiring because I would have to look at all the symbols under a category to find something similar. Additionally, with words that were damaged, it was practically impossible to find the hittite word (especially when the starting stroke was damaged). I found this difficult for the 'hook' starting symbols too. Some transcriptions are unclear about if they start with diagonal strokes, and this system was really difficult to use to make sense of the actual starting stroke.
Z Suggested improvements: Possibly lots of experience using this system previously. I don't think a beginner could find this system easy to use.
